title: Building and Distributing CLI Tools
content:
Rust produces static binaries with no runtime dependencies, making distribution simple. Users just need the binary.

For distribution: publish to crates.io for `cargo install` support, create GitHub releases with cross-compiled binaries, or package for system package managers.

Cross-compilation lets you build for Linux, macOS, and Windows from a single machine. The cross tool makes this easy. Use cargo build --release for optimized production binaries.

codeExamples.code:
# Cargo.toml
# [profile.release]
# opt-level = 3
# lto = true          # Link-time optimization
# codegen-units = 1   # Better optimization, slower compile
# strip = true        # Strip debug symbols
# panic = "abort"     # Smaller binary (no unwinding)

# Build:
# cargo build --release
# Binary is at: target/release/my-tool

# Cargo.toml metadata for publishing
# [package]
# name = "my-tool"
# version = "1.0.0"
# edition = "2021"
# authors = ["Your Name <you@example.com>"]
# description = "A useful command-line tool"
# license = "MIT"
# repository = "https://github.com/you/my-tool"
# keywords = ["cli", "tool"]
# categories = ["command-line-utilities"]
#
# Then:
# cargo login <your-api-token>
# cargo publish
#
# Users install with:
# cargo install my-tool

# Install cross: cargo install cross
# Build for different targets:

# Linux (x86_64, musl for static linking)
# cross build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl

# macOS (Intel)
# cross build --release --target x86_64-apple-darwin

# macOS (Apple Silicon)
# cross build --release --target aarch64-apple-darwin

# Windows
# cross build --release --target x86_64-pc-windows-gnu

keyPoints: Enable LTO and strip symbols for smaller binaries
cargo install my-tool installs from crates.io
Use cross for easy cross-compilation to other platforms
musl target on Linux produces fully static binaries
GitHub Actions can automate cross-platform builds and releases
Include version, license, and description in Cargo.toml for publishing
